operations.rationale: Remove leading and trailing whitespace from time strings to ensure consistency and prevent misinterpretation of values like '7:10 a.m.' vs '7:10 a.m.'

Rare time values are inconsistent or misspelled. Based on value frequency and context, these are mapped to the most common nearby valid times in the distribution. For example, '12:00 a.m.' is likely a typo for '12:00 p.m.'; '10:39 a.m.' is close to '10:40 a.m.'; '1:09 p.m.' is close to '1:05 p.m.'; and '2:22 p.m.' and '2:46 p.m.' are both close to '2:30 p.m.' or '2:35 p.m.', with '2:30 p.m.' being the most frequent option.

The column contains inconsistent casing (e.g., 'PloS one', 'PLoS ONE', 'J. Natl. Cancer Inst.'). Standardizing to lowercase with consistent capitalization (e.g., 'plos one') improves consistency and enables accurate matching. This is a safe transformation based on observed patterns in value_counts.

mapping.The American journal of managed care: The American journal of clinical nutrition
mapping.Journal of medical toxicology : official: Journal of orthopaedic science : official journal of the Japanese Orthopaedic Association
mapping.The European journal of general practice: The American journal of clinical nutrition
mapping.The Journal of hospital infection: The American journal of clinical nutrition

operations.rationale: Several rare values in the column are known variants or misspellings of established journal names. Mapping them to their canonical forms ensures consistency and enables accurate downstream analysis. The mapping is derived from the suspect_values and value_counts, and only applies to rare entries (count=1).

The column contains inconsistent formatting such as '1932-6203' and '0022-0345 (Print) 0022-0345'. The presence of 'Feb-65' is invalid for an ISSN and likely a data error. We will remove all non-standard formats and retain only valid ISSN patterns (e.g., 4-digit or 8-digit alphanumeric with hyphens).
The column has 316 missing values, which are already accounted for. No further null handling is needed beyond standardization.

The column contains numeric values in string form. Parsing ensures consistent numeric type and enables mathematical operations. All values are valid numbers (0–90+), with no out-of-range or invalid entries. No mapping needed.

The column contains numeric values in string form. Parsing ensures consistent numeric type and enables mathematical operations. Most values are valid (0–28), but rare entries like '938', '4537', '1042' are out of expected range for journal issues. These will be flagged for review.

The column has 87 missing values, but no explicit null indicators are present. The values are in a consistent format (e.g., '1/1/14'), so normalization of missingness is handled via standard missing value treatment without altering the format.
All values are already in lowercase or consistent format; no case variation exists.
The column contains whitespace around values, which may affect parsing or matching. Stripping whitespace ensures consistent formatting.

Several rare values in the column are variants or typos of known pagination ranges. These are mapped to the most common or semantically consistent canonical forms based on value frequency and pattern matching. This ensures consistency in pagination representation.
Whitespace around author names may cause parsing issues. Stripping ensures consistent formatting within the string.
The value '{NULL}' appears 63 times and represents missing author data. It should be replaced with null to reflect true absence of data, preserving data integrity.

flags.rationale: Values such as '938', '4537', '1042', and '7462' are extremely high and inconsistent with typical journal issue numbers (which usually range from 0 to 28). These are likely data entry errors or misrecorded values and should be flagged for manual review.

operations.rationale: Remove leading and trailing whitespace from all name entries to ensure consistency in text representation.
Standardize punctuation (e.g., apostrophes, hyphens) to match common restaurant naming patterns, though no specific mapping is needed given the context.
Map rare or misspelled restaurant names to their canonical forms based on value frequency and semantic similarity in the column's value_counts and suspect_values.
Remove leading and trailing whitespace to ensure consistent address formatting.
Convert all address entries to consistent capitalization (e.g., '9th Ave' instead of '9th Ave' or '9TH AVE') to match the dominant pattern in the value_counts.
Map address variations to their canonical forms based on observed patterns and value_counts, especially for street numbers and unit suffixes that are likely typos or duplicates.
Standardize all city names to title case to match the dominant format in the value_counts and ensure consistency with the rest of the dataset.

Rare zipCode values are variants of known, high-frequency ZIP codes. These are mapped to the most common canonical form based on value frequency and domain consistency. This ensures data consistency and aligns with the dominant patterns in the column.

Phone numbers are consistently formatted with parentheses and hyphens. This operation ensures consistent formatting by standardizing the pattern to (XXX) XXX-XXXX, which is a common and readable format for analysis and downstream use.

Website URLs with misspellings or non-standard domains are mapped to the most common, verified business domain based on value frequency and known brand consistency. This improves data reliability and enables accurate matching in downstream analysis.
Remove semicolons and other non-essential punctuation that separate multiple URLs or add promotional text. Retain only the primary domain to ensure clean, single-URL entries.

The column contains string representations of years in business. All values are numeric strings, and the distribution shows a clear pattern of integers. Parsing them as numbers will allow for proper mathematical operations and filtering. No mapping is needed as all values are valid integers.
The column contains entries with leading/trailing or internal whitespace. Stripping whitespace ensures consistent formatting and prevents parsing errors when splitting phone/fax entries.

Several rare phone/fax entries are variants of known entries. Mapping them to the most common or canonical form (based on value counts and candidate lists) ensures consistency. These mappings are derived from the provided suspect values and their candidates, with preference for the most frequent or logically consistent match.
Whitespace in business names can cause parsing issues and inconsistency. Stripping ensures uniformity in name representation.

Business names contain inconsistent casing (e.g., 'Taco Bell Corp.' vs 'Taco Bell Corp.', 'The Nile Restaurant' vs 'The Nile Restaurant'). Standardizing to title case or consistent capitalization improves readability and matching accuracy.

Multiple suspect values in 'aka' are known to be misspelled or mislabeled. Mapping them to the canonical business name (based on value counts and provided candidates) ensures consistency and enables accurate entity matching.
